Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd have bursts where I'd binge-watch all the lecture videos and then go weeks without touching anything. I learn best by doing things with my hands, so I'd often just skim the online stuff and then try to work through problems on my own. I never really had a set schedule—it was more like studying when the mood struck me or when I suddenly realized something was due.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go through phases where I was really into the course material, then completely drift away when something else caught my attention. Deadlines were definitely the main thing that would snap me back into action. Some weeks I'd study maybe an hour total, other weeks I'd cram in way more when assignments were looming.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about a deadline or pure curiosity when I remembered something interesting from a previous lecture. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything new, kind of like scrolling social media out of habit. Other times I wouldn't log in for ages until I got that panic feeling about missing something important.
Q4: For a while there, checking the LMS became part of my morning routine—I'd log in with my coffee just to see what was happening. That happened after I missed a deadline once and felt terrible about it. But honestly, that habit faded after a few weeks when I got distracted by other stuff going on in my life. It was never really a solid routine for me.
Q5: The videos were actually really helpful because I could pause them and try things out myself as I watched. I also liked the discussion forums because seeing other students' questions made me realize I wasn't the only one confused about certain things. The interactive practice problems were great too since I'm a hands-on learner and just reading doesn't stick for me.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and PDFs. I'd download them with good intentions but they'd just sit in a folder on my laptop. The supplementary resources felt overwhelming—there was just so much, and I couldn't figure out what was actually important versus what was just extra. I also rarely touched the peer review activities because they felt like busywork.
Q7: If I could see how something connected to the assignments or exams, I was way more likely to actually do it. Activities that let me practice and get immediate feedback felt worthwhile because I could tell if I was understanding things. Anything that felt too theoretical or disconnected from real application just didn't hold my attention.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could ignore, which I usually did. Required ones had this weight to them that made me actually prioritize them, even if I was cramming last minute. I think knowing something was optional gave me permission to skip it, especially when I was feeling lazy or had other things going on. The required stuff at least got done eventually.
Q9: Oh definitely. I'd download videos and watch them on my tablet while doing other things around my apartment. I also used YouTube tutorials and external websites a lot because sometimes they explained things differently and it would finally click. My notes were all in a physical notebook because writing by hand helps me remember better than typing.
Q10: So many times! I'd spend hours working through practice problems in my notebook or building little projects to test what I'd learned, but none of that showed up anywhere online. I'd also have study sessions with a friend from another section where we'd work through concepts together at a coffee shop. The LMS would just show I was inactive when I was actually being pretty productive.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video or PDF once, and then that was it—I'd study from the downloaded version for weeks. Sometimes I'd rewatch the same video multiple times offline while commuting or exercising. The LMS probably thought I only looked at things once when really I was reviewing them repeatedly.
Q12: There was a period mid-semester where I just completely checked out. I think I was surprised by how much I'd let slide and felt overwhelmed trying to catch up. The CBT sessions I did actually helped me work through some of that mental block. It wasn't that the course was too hard, I just lost momentum and struggled to get it back.
Q13: When I had other assignments from different courses piling up, the LMS for this course definitely took a backseat. Fatigue was a big one too—I'd log in planning to watch a lecture and then just not have the mental energy to actually focus. I have good resources and internet access, so it wasn't about that, it was more about my own scattered attention.
Q14: Yeah, there were plenty of times I'd have a video playing while I was scrolling my phone or doing something else. I'd technically be 'watching' but not actually absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd click through materials just to mark them as complete without really engaging. It's like my body was there but my mind was somewhere else entirely.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they felt like a chore. Even when I knew participating would help my grade, I'd put it off until the last possible moment or sometimes miss the window entirely. I think I avoided things that required me to put my thoughts out there publicly—it felt vulnerable somehow, even though I knew it was important.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd have this pattern of ignoring everything until about 24-48 hours before something was due, then going into panic mode. The LMS would probably show these huge spikes of activity right before deadlines and then nothing for days. It wasn't healthy but it's how I operated.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it motivated me to engage more with the next assignment. But honestly, I didn't always check my feedback right away—sometimes I'd avoid looking at it because I was nervous about what it said. The feedback that actually changed my behavior was specific and showed me exactly what to fix, not just generic comments.
Q18: The course was structured with a lot of flexibility, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work at my own pace when motivated, bad because I could also completely ignore things when I wasn't. If there had been more regular check-ins or smaller deadlines, I probably would have stayed more consistent instead of drifting.
Q19: The fact that I could access everything anytime made it easy to procrastinate because there was no urgency. Having so many resources available was actually overwhelming—I didn't know where to start sometimes. What helped was when modules were clearly organized with a suggested order, so I didn't have to figure out the path myself.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—exploring, clicking around, watching videos, but then getting stuck when it comes to actually producing work. There's a big gap between consuming content and creating something from it. They might also be perfectionists who keep reviewing material but never feel ready to submit anything.
Q21: That's basically my situation. I did well on exams because I'm actually pretty good at understanding concepts once I engage with them, even if my attendance and assignment completion were spotty. A lot of my real learning happened offline or in concentrated bursts that wouldn't show up in regular LMS activity. The grade doesn't always match the logged hours.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on, but they completely miss the quality of that engagement. They can't tell if you're actually thinking deeply or just going through the motions. They also miss all the offline work, the conversations with classmates, the moments of understanding that happen away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just complete it. For me, it's when I find myself thinking about course concepts outside of study time or wanting to explore topics further. It's not about hours logged or boxes checked—it's about whether the material actually gets into your head and stays there.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I actually found this course pretty interesting when I let myself engage with it. It wouldn't capture the hands-on experiments I did on my own or the late-night conversations about course topics with friends. It also wouldn't show that my inconsistent attendance wasn't about not caring—it was about struggling to maintain momentum even when I wanted to.
